narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 43.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile shows solid aspects, like a high family income bracket and a full-time study commitment, alongside an LSAT score of 36—close to the typical student's 36.7—and being male, which is common among applicants. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 is notably below the average of 3.21, and your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 falls short of the usual 5.71, with your Year 3 decile of 1 being especially low compared to the typical 5.53. As a Black applicant, you're in a smaller group, making up just 4.6% of the dataset versus 83.8% White students. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also contributing toward that unfavorable outcome. Your high family income followed closely, unexpectedly nudging the prediction in the same direction despite its general positivity. Your racial background had a notable but slightly lesser impact, pushing toward failure as well. Your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA each played supporting roles in reinforcing the risk of failing the bar. Factors like your full-time status and gender had the smallest influence, still leaning slightly toward the failure prediction. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower academic deciles and GPA stood out as bigger drags than your near-average LSAT. To improve your future chances, focus on bolstering your academic performance—perhaps through targeted study strategies or additional coursework to mimic stronger decile rankings and raise metrics like your GPA equivalent in law school. Retaking the LSAT for a boost could help too, as it's a changeable factor that carries weight. With dedication to these areas, you could shift the model's view toward seeing you as a likely bar passer. Keep pushing forward; many overcome similar hurdles with focused effort.
